items.note: 月 = moon/month. The seven weekdays map directly onto the seven classical planets: Sun (日), Moon (月), Mars (火), Mercury (水), Jupiter (木), Venus (金), Saturn (土). This system reached Japan via China from Babylonian astrology. 月 alone reads 'tsuki' (moon, the celestial object) but reads 'gatsu' in month names and 'getsu' in compound words like 月曜日. 毎月 (maitsuki) = every month. 月光 (gekkō) = moonlight.

火 = fire. Tuesday corresponds to Mars (火星, kasei = Fire Star). 火 reads 'hi' when standalone (火 = fire, flame) and 'ka' in compounds. 火山 (kazan) = volcano (fire + mountain). 花火 (hanabi) = fireworks (flower + fire — the sparks bloom like flowers). 火事 (kaji) = fire/blaze (the dangerous kind). 火曜日 is often abbreviated 火 on calendars.

水 = water. Wednesday is Mercury (水星, suisei = Water Star). 水 reads 'mizu' standalone but 'sui' in compounds. 水族館 (suizokukan) = aquarium (water + tribe + building). 水泳 (suiei) = swimming. 水道 (suidō) = water supply/tap. 洪水 (kōzui) = flood. The 水 radical (氵, three drops, left-side form) appears in hundreds of water-related kanji: 海 (sea), 泳 (swim), 湖 (lake), 泣 (cry).

木 = tree/wood. Thursday is Jupiter (木星, mokusei = Wood Star). 木 reads 'ki' or 'ko' standalone (the tree, a counter for trees) and 'moku' in formal compounds. 木造 (mokuzō) = wooden structure. 木曜日 → 木 on calendars. The kanji 林 (hayashi = grove) is two 木, and 森 (mori = forest) is three 木 — the number of trees shows the density. 木 as a radical appears in 机 (desk), 椅子 (chair), 橋 (bridge) — all wooden things.

金 = gold / metal / money. Friday is Venus (金星, kinsei = Gold/Metal Star). 金 reads 'kin' in compounds (お金, okane = money; 金メダル, kin medaru = gold medal; 金庫, kinko = safe/vault) and 'kane' in some words (お金, 鐘 = bell). 金曜日 is stereotypically the day people go out — 花金 (hanaking, informal) = 'flower Friday', the happy Friday before the weekend. 金属 (kinzoku) = metal (the material).

土 = earth / soil / ground. Saturday is Saturn (土星, dosei = Earth Star). 土 reads 'tsuchi' standalone (the soil, the ground, the earth element) and 'do' or 'to' in compounds. 土地 (tochi) = land/plot. お土産 (omiyage) = souvenir (literally 'earth product' — something characteristic of a place). 土台 (dodai) = foundation. 土砂降り (doshaburi) = heavy downpour. The 土 radical appears in 地 (ground), 場 (place), 型 (mold).

日 = sun / day. Sunday is the Sun (太陽, taiyō). 日 has three readings: 'hi' (the sun: 日の出 = hinode = sunrise), 'nichi' in formal compounds (日曜日, 毎日 mainichi = every day), and 'ka' when counting days (三日 = mikka = 3rd/three days). 日本 (Nihon/Nippon) = Japan, 'origin of the sun'. 日記 (nikki) = diary. 日程 (nittei) = schedule. The 日 radical is extremely common: 明 (bright = sun+moon), 時 (time), 晴 (clear weather).

月 in month names reads 'gatsu' (not 'tsuki'). January is 新年 (shinnen = New Year) month. 正月 (shōgatsu) = the New Year season/holiday — note 正 here, not 一! The most important holiday in Japan; many businesses close for the first week. 初詣 (hatsumōde) = first shrine visit of the year. Numbers in months use on'yomi (Chinese readings): ichi, ni, san, etc. — with key exceptions at 4, 7, and 9.

The shortest month. 節分 (setsubun, February 3) is the traditional last day of winter — people throw roasted soybeans (豆まき, mame-maki) shouting 鬼は外！福は内！ (Oni wa soto! Fuku wa uchi! = Demons out! Luck in!). バレンタインデー (barentain dē, Valentine's Day) has a unique twist: women give chocolate to men, and men reciprocate on ホワイトデー (White Day, March 14).

ひな祭り (hinamatsuri, March 3) = Doll Festival / Girls' Day. Families display tiered platforms of ornamental dolls representing the Imperial court. Late March sees the beginning of 桜 (sakura = cherry blossom) season in southern Japan — the blossoms move north over six weeks, tracked by the 桜前線 (sakura zensen = cherry blossom front). Spring equinox (春分の日, shunbun no hi) is a national holiday.

四 reads 'shi' in month names, NOT 'yon' (the kun'yomi). This is a crucial distinction — 四月 is shi-gatsu, not yon-gatsu. April is the start of the Japanese school year, fiscal year, and many company financial calendars (入学式, nyūgakushiki = school entrance ceremony). Cherry blossoms peak in Tokyo in early April. ゴールデンウィーク (Golden Week) begins late April. 四 = 4: the on'yomi 'shi' sounds like 死 (death), making 4 an unlucky number.

ゴールデンウィーク (Golden Week, late April to May 5) clusters four national holidays: 昭和の日 (April 29), 憲法記念日 (May 3, Constitution Day), みどりの日 (May 4, Greenery Day), こどもの日 (May 5, Children's Day). Japan effectively shuts down — book travel far in advance. こどもの日 features 鯉のぼり (koinobori) — carp-shaped streamers flown outside to wish strength for children (carp swim upstream against current).

June marks 梅雨 (tsuyu = rainy season), which runs roughly from early June to mid-July in Honshu. 梅 = plum (the plums ripen during this rainy period), 雨 = rain. 梅雨 is written with the kanji for plum + rain. Humidity is high and it rains most days — not a tourist favourite, but 紫陽花 (ajisai = hydrangeas) bloom beautifully in the grey light. Hokkaido largely escapes the rainy season.

七 reads 'shichi' in month names, NOT 'nana'. 七月七日 = 七夕 (tanabata, July 7) — the Star Festival, celebrating the annual meeting of the star deities Orihime (Vega, the weaver) and Hikoboshi (Altair, the cowherd) across the Milky Way. People write wishes on coloured paper and hang them on bamboo. After tsuyu ends (mid-July), summer heat begins in earnest. お盆 (obon) preparations start.

The hottest month. お盆 (obon, mid-August) is the Buddhist period for honouring ancestors — families return to hometowns, making it Japan's busiest travel season. 盆踊り (bon odori) = traditional dancing during obon. August 15 is 終戦記念日 (shūsen kinenbi = end of war memorial day). 打ち水 (uchimizu) = sprinkling water on pavement to cool it down — a Edo-period heat-beating technique still used today.

九 reads 'ku' in month names, NOT 'kyuu'. Typhoon season peaks in September — Japan sits in the main typhoon track from the Pacific. 秋分の日 (shūbun no hi = autumn equinox, around Sept 23) is a national holiday. September signals the end of intense summer heat, though humidity lingers. 九 is sometimes considered unlucky: 'ku' also sounds like 苦 (ku = suffering/pain).

Autumn is considered Japan's best travel season: mild temperatures, low humidity, and beginning 紅葉 (kōyō = autumn foliage). 紅 = crimson, 葉 = leaf. Like the sakura front in spring, there is a 紅葉前線 (kōyō zensen = autumn colour front) moving from north to south. 体育の日 (taiiku no hi = Health & Sports Day, second Monday of October) commemorates the 1964 Tokyo Olympics opening. Halloween has become very popular in Japan, especially in Tokyo.

紅葉 peaks in most of Honshu during November. 七五三 (shichi-go-san, November 15) — children aged 7 (七), 5 (五), and 3 (三) are taken to shrines to pray for their health and growth, dressed in kimono. The name literally means '7-5-3'. 文化の日 (bunka no hi = Culture Day, November 3) celebrates the arts and academia — many museums offer free admission.

クリスマス (kurisumasu) in Japan is celebrated as a romantic couples' holiday rather than a family one — KFC has become the traditional Christmas dinner (a marketing campaign from 1974 that became cultural institution). 大晦日 (ōmisoka, December 31) = New Year's Eve. Temples ring their bells 108 times at midnight (除夜の鐘, joya no kane) — 108 represents the 108 worldly desires in Buddhism. 忘年会 (bōnenkai) = year-end parties ('forget the year' drinking parties).

週 (shuu) = week, 末 (matsu/sue) = end/tip. 週 contains the 辶 radical (movement) + 周 (circumference, cycle) — a week is a cycle. Confusable: 週末 (shuumatsu = weekend) vs 終末 (shuumatsu = end times/apocalypse) — same pronunciation, very different meaning! 先週末 = last weekend, 来週末 = next weekend.

平 (hei) = flat / ordinary / peaceful. 日 = day. A 'flat/ordinary day' — i.e., not a holiday. 平日は混んでいます (heijitsu wa konde imasu) = 'It's crowded on weekdays'. 平 also appears in 平和 (heiwa = peace), 平均 (heikin = average), 平仮名 (hiragana — 'ordinary/flat kana', as opposed to 片仮名 = katakana, 'partial kana').

今 (kon/ima) = now/this. 週 = week. 今 changes pronunciation depending on what follows: 今週 (kon-shuu), 今月 (kon-getsu = this month), 今年 (ko-toshi = this year) — note 今年 breaks the pattern and uses the kun'yomi 'toshi' for 年. 今 standalone = ima (now, at this moment). 今日 (kyō) = today — also irregular, not 'konnichi' (though こんにちは greeting comes from こんにち + は).

来 (rai) = coming / arrival. 来週 = next week, 来月 (raigetsu) = next month, 来年 (rainen) = next year. 来 also means 'to come': 来る (kuru). 来 contains the idea of something arriving — a crop coming in, a harvest. 再来週 (saraishuu) = the week after next (再 = again/re-).

先 (sen) = before / previous / ahead. 先週 = last week, 先月 (sengetsu) = last month, 去年 (kyonen) = last year — note that 'last year' uses 去 (去年), not 先. 先 appears in 先生 (sensei = teacher, literally 'one born before') and 先輩 (senpai = senior). 先 implies priority and precedence: the one who went ahead of you.

祝 (shuku) = celebration/congratulation. 日 = day. Japan has 16 national holidays — one of the most in the world. If a holiday falls on Sunday, the following Monday becomes a 振替休日 (furikae kyūjitsu = substitute holiday). Three clusters create mini-holidays: ゴールデンウィーク (April-May), シルバーウィーク (September), and 年末年始 (year-end/New Year). お祝い (oiwai) = celebration/gift.

Japanese dates go from largest to smallest unit: year → month → day. 2025年4月1日 = April 1st, 2025. 年 (nen/toshi) = year, 月 (gatsu) = month, 日 (nichi/ka) = day. Days 1–10 and 14, 20, 24 have irregular readings: 一日 (tsuitachi), 二日 (futsuka), 三日 (mikka), 四日 (yokka), 五日 (itsuka), 六日 (muika), 七日 (nanoka), 八日 (youka), 九日 (kokonoka), 十日 (tooka), 十四日 (juuyokka), 二十日 (hatsuka), 二十四日 (nijuuyokka). These are ancient native Japanese (kun'yomi) counting words, not the usual ichi/ni/san system.
